Command-Line Interface (CLI)

Source: vignettes/cli-usage.Rmd
cli-usage.Rmd

Overview

scConvert provides two CLI entry points for file-to-file conversion:

  1. C binary (scconvert): Fast streaming conversion between HDF5 formats (h5ad, h5seurat, h5mu) without loading data into memory.
  2. R backend (sc_cli_convert()): Universal any-to-any conversion supporting all six formats (h5ad, h5seurat, h5mu, loom, rds, zarr).

The sc_cli_convert() function automatically uses the C binary for HDF5 format pairs and falls back to R for other formats.

Supported formats

Format Extension Notes
AnnData HDF5 .h5ad C binary + R
SeuratDisk HDF5 .h5seurat C binary + R
MuData HDF5 .h5mu C binary + R
Loom HDF5 .loom R backend
R serialized .rds R backend
AnnData Zarr .zarr R backend

Any source format can be converted to any destination format.

Building the C binary

The C binary is optional and provides faster HDF5-to-HDF5 conversion. It requires HDF5 development headers.

On macOS with Homebrew:

brew install hdf5

cd /path/to/scConvert/src
make

On Ubuntu/Debian:

sudo apt-get install libhdf5-dev

cd /path/to/scConvert/src
make

This produces the scconvert binary in the src/ directory. You can copy it to a directory on your PATH:

cp src/scconvert ~/bin/  # or any directory on your PATH

Usage from R

The sc_cli_convert() function is the primary interface for programmatic file-to-file conversion:

library(scConvert)

# Any-to-any conversion
sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.h5seurat")
sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.loom")
sc_cli_convert("data.loom", "data.zarr")
sc_cli_convert("data.rds", "data.h5ad")
sc_cli_convert("data.zarr", "data.rds", overwrite = TRUE)

Options

Argument Description Default
assay Assay/modality name "RNA"
gzip Compression level (0-9, C binary only) 4
overwrite Overwrite existing output FALSE
verbose Show progress messages TRUE

Usage from the command line

C binary (HDF5 formats only) 

scconvert <input> <output> [options]

The conversion direction is auto-detected from file extensions.

Flag Description Default
--assay <name> Assay/modality name RNA
--gzip <level> Compression level (0-9) 1
--overwrite Overwrite existing output file off
--quiet Suppress progress messages off
--version Print version and exit
--help Print help and exit

R backend (all formats)

For formats not supported by the C binary (loom, rds, zarr), use the R wrapper script installed with the package:

Rscript -e 'scConvert::sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.loom")'
Rscript -e 'scConvert::sc_cli_convert("data.loom", "data.zarr")'

Or use the bundled wrapper script:

Rscript $(Rscript -e 'cat(system.file("bin", "scconvert", package="scConvert"))') \
  data.h5ad data.loom --assay RNA

Examples

Basic conversions 

# HDF5 format pairs (uses C binary when available)
scconvert data.h5ad data.h5seurat
scconvert data.h5seurat data.h5ad --assay RNA --gzip 6

# MuData conversions
scconvert multimodal.h5mu multimodal.h5seurat
scconvert multimodal.h5mu rna_only.h5ad --assay rna

Any-to-any from R 

# Convert between any formats
sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.loom")
sc_cli_convert("data.loom", "data.zarr")
sc_cli_convert("data.zarr", "data.rds")
sc_cli_convert("data.rds", "data.h5seurat")

Batch conversion 

# Convert all h5ad files to loom
h5ad_files <- list.files(".", pattern = "\\.h5ad$", full.names = TRUE)
for (f in h5ad_files) {
  out <- sub("\\.h5ad$", ".loom", f)
  sc_cli_convert(f, out)
}

Accelerated Convert()

When the scConvert.use_cli option is set, Convert() will prefer the C binary for supported HDF5 format pairs:

options(scConvert.use_cli = TRUE)
Convert("large_dataset.h5ad", dest = "h5seurat")

Visualization: CLI output in R and Python

A key advantage of the CLI is that converted files are valid in both R and Python ecosystems. Below we demonstrate a round-trip: convert a Seurat h5Seurat file to h5ad with the CLI, then visualize in both Seurat and scanpy.

Setup: create test data and convert 

library(scConvert)
library(Seurat)
#> Loading required package: SeuratObject
#> Loading required package: sp
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'SeuratObject'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, t

# Load bundled PBMC dataset
obj <- readRDS(system.file("testdata", "pbmc_small.rds", package = "scConvert"))

# Save as h5Seurat
h5s_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".h5Seurat")
SaveH5Seurat(obj, h5s_path, overwrite = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)

# Convert h5Seurat -> h5ad using the CLI
h5ad_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".h5ad")
sc_cli_convert(h5s_path, h5ad_path, verbose = FALSE)
#> Validating h5Seurat file
#> [1] TRUE
cat("Converted:", basename(h5s_path), "->", basename(h5ad_path), "\n")
#> Converted: file152b240aea9ba.h5Seurat -> file152b2b091383.h5ad

Visualize in Seurat (R)

Load the CLI-produced h5ad back into R and make a UMAP plot:

library(ggplot2)

# Load CLI h5ad output
obj_cli <- LoadH5AD(h5ad_path, verbose = FALSE)

# Verify data integrity
cat("Cells:", ncol(obj_cli), "| Genes:", nrow(obj_cli), "\n")
#> Cells: 214 | Genes: 2000
cat("Reductions:", paste(Reductions(obj_cli), collapse = ", "), "\n")
#> Reductions: pca, umap

# Side-by-side: original vs CLI round-trip
p1 <- DimPlot(obj, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_annotations") +
  ggtitle("Original Seurat object") + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
p2 <- DimPlot(obj_cli, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_annotations") +
  ggtitle("After CLI h5Seurat -> h5ad -> R") + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
p1 + p2

The UMAP coordinates and cluster annotations are preserved exactly through the CLI conversion.

Visualize in scanpy (Python)

The same CLI-produced h5ad file can be loaded directly in scanpy:

import scanpy as sc
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

adata = sc.read_h5ad(r.h5ad_path)
print(f"Shape: {adata.shape}")
print(f"Embeddings: {list(adata.obsm.keys())}")

# Compute neighbors and cluster from the preserved PCA
sc.pp.neighbors(adata, use_rep="X_pca")
sc.tl.leiden(adata, resolution=0.5)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

# Plot preserved UMAP with original annotations
sc.pl.embedding(adata, basis="X_umap", color="seurat_annotations",
                title="CLI h5ad in scanpy\n(original annotations)",
                ax=axes[0], show=False)

# Plot with scanpy-computed leiden clusters
sc.pl.embedding(adata, basis="X_umap", color="leiden",
                title="CLI h5ad in scanpy\n(scanpy leiden clusters)",
                ax=axes[1], show=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("scanpy_cli_viz.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.close()

The preserved PCA embeddings allow scanpy to compute neighbors and clusters without re-running dimensionality reduction. Cell annotations, expression values, and all metadata survive the conversion.

Verify: convert back to h5Seurat 

# Convert h5ad back to h5Seurat
h5s_roundtrip <- tempfile(fileext = ".h5Seurat")
sc_cli_convert(h5ad_path, h5s_roundtrip, verbose = FALSE)
#> Adding X_pca as cell embeddings for pca
#> Adding X_umap as cell embeddings for umap
#> [1] TRUE

obj_rt <- LoadH5Seurat(h5s_roundtrip, verbose = FALSE)
#> Validating h5Seurat file
#> Warning: Layer 'data' is empty
cat("Round-trip PCA match:",
    all.equal(Embeddings(obj, "pca"), Embeddings(obj_rt, "pca"),
              check.attributes = FALSE), "\n")
#> Round-trip PCA match: TRUE
cat("Round-trip counts match:",
    identical(as.matrix(GetAssayData(obj, layer = "counts")),
              as.matrix(GetAssayData(obj_rt, layer = "counts"))), "\n")
#> Round-trip counts match: TRUE

Performance

The C binary uses streaming chunk-based I/O and avoids materializing the full dataset in memory. Key optimizations:

  • Direct chunk copy: When source and destination use the same compression, compressed chunks are transferred without decompression/recompression.
  • HDF5 native group copy: Metadata groups (uns, obsp, layers) are copied with H5Ocopy() in a single operation.
  • Low default compression: Gzip level 1 provides fast writes with only ~10% size increase vs level 4.

For non-HDF5 formats, the R backend loads data into a Seurat object as an intermediate step. This requires more memory but supports the full format matrix.

Benchmark results

The most common workflows are loading h5ad into Seurat (LoadH5AD) and writing back to h5ad (SeuratToH5AD). The table below shows performance on synthetic sparse h5ad files (5% density, 20,000 genes). RDS round-trips include readRDS/saveRDS time:

scConvert performance (median wall-clock seconds, 20K genes)
Cells h5ad → Seurat Seurat → h5ad RDS → h5ad h5ad → RDS
1,000 0.25 s 0.58 s 1.7 s 1.1 s
10,000 1.15 s 2.7 s 3.0 s 9.2 s
100,000 7.5 s 24.7 s 28.0 s 89.7 s

LoadH5AD and SeuratToH5AD are the fastest paths because they work directly with HDF5 — no intermediate serialization. The RDS paths include R object serialization overhead; h5ad → RDS is dominated by saveRDS() compressing the Seurat object.

For datasets exceeding available memory, LoadH5AD() supports on-disk loading via BPCells:

# On-disk loading — minimal memory footprint
obj <- LoadH5AD("large_dataset.h5ad", use.bpcells = TRUE)

C binary for batch HDF5 conversion

For batch pipelines that convert between HDF5 formats without needing an in-memory Seurat object, the C binary provides an additional speedup by copying compressed chunks directly between files:

Direction 1K 10K 100K
h5ad → h5seurat 0.018 s 0.030 s 0.16 s
h5seurat → h5ad 0.011 s 0.014 s 0.04 s

Why is the C binary so fast? It uses H5Dread_chunk / H5Dwrite_chunk to transfer compressed data blocks directly — no decompression, no R memory allocation. This is ~50× faster than the R API but only converts between HDF5 formats (h5ad, h5seurat, h5mu). For h5ad ↔︎ Seurat/RDS, use the R API.

Additional Format Examples

Beyond HDF5 formats, sc_cli_convert() supports Zarr, RDS, and SingleCellExperiment (SCE) through the R backend.

Zarr conversion

Zarr is a chunked, compressed array store popular in cloud-native workflows. scConvert reads and writes Zarr v2 stores following the AnnData on-disk specification.

# h5ad -> Zarr (via R API or CLI)
Convert("data.h5ad", dest = "data.zarr", overwrite = TRUE)
sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.zarr", overwrite = TRUE)

# Zarr -> Seurat
obj <- LoadZarr("data.zarr", verbose = TRUE)

# Zarr -> other formats
Convert("data.zarr", dest = "roundtrip.h5ad", overwrite = TRUE)
Convert("data.zarr", dest = "data.rds", overwrite = TRUE)
Convert("data.zarr", dest = "data.loom", overwrite = TRUE)

RDS and SingleCellExperiment conversion

RDS files store native R objects, preserving the complete Seurat object with all assays, reductions, and metadata. SingleCellExperiment (SCE) objects can be converted through Seurat’s built-in as.SingleCellExperiment() and as.Seurat() methods.

# h5ad -> RDS
Convert("data.h5ad", dest = "data.rds", overwrite = TRUE)
sc_cli_convert("data.h5ad", "data.rds", overwrite = TRUE)

# RDS -> h5ad
Convert("data.rds", dest = "data.h5ad", overwrite = TRUE)

# Seurat <-> SCE
seurat_obj <- LoadH5AD("data.h5ad")
sce <- as.SingleCellExperiment(seurat_obj)
seurat_from_sce <- as.Seurat(sce)
SeuratToH5AD(seurat_from_sce, "from_sce.h5ad", overwrite = TRUE)

Multi-step conversion chain

All conversions route through Seurat as a universal intermediate, so formats can be chained freely:

# h5ad -> Zarr -> RDS -> h5ad (three formats, three hops)
Convert("data.h5ad", dest = "data.zarr", overwrite = TRUE)
Convert("data.zarr", dest = "data.rds", overwrite = TRUE)
Convert("data.rds", dest = "roundtrip.h5ad", overwrite = TRUE)

Format comparison

Feature h5ad h5Seurat h5mu Loom Zarr RDS SCE
File-based Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes (directory) Yes No (in-memory)
Python interop Native No Native Yes Native No No
R interop Via scConvert Native Via scConvert Via scConvert Via scConvert Native Native (Bioc)
Multimodal Via layers Via assays Native No Via layers Via assays Via altExps
Spatial data Yes Yes Limited No Yes Yes Limited
Cloud-native No No No No Yes No No

Session Info 

sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
#> Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin20
#> Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.3
#> 
#> Matrix products: default
#> BLAS:   /System/Library/Frameworks/Accelerate.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/vecLib.framework/Versions/A/libBLAS.dylib 
#> LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.5-arm64/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;  LAPACK version 3.12.1
